title: Ruth 3
subtitle: Naomi's Plan; the Threshing Floor; Ruth's Proposal; Boaz's Oath

timeline_link.text: See on Timeline — Ruth and Boaz

sections.header: Verses 1–6 — Naomi's Plan: The Threshing Floor

heb.paragraph: Naomi's desire to find "a home" (mānôaḥ) for Ruth (v.1) uses the same word she used of the LORD's gift to the daughters-in-law in 1:9 — "may the LORD grant you rest (mānôaḥ)." The word signifies more than shelter: it is covenant security, the settled condition of one whose future is secured. Naomi is now actively working to bring about what she had previously only prayed for — she has moved from lament to agency.

The threshing floor (v.2-3) is a liminal space in the OT — a place of agricultural abundance (Hos 9:1-2), of legal transaction (2 Sam 24:18-25), and in this narrative, of covenant negotiation. At night, after the celebratory harvest meal, the workers sleep on the threshing floor to guard the grain. Naomi's plan places Ruth in a space where legal-covenant action can occur, under cover of both darkness and the legitimate social custom of workers sleeping near the harvest.

Naomi's instruction that Ruth should "uncover his feet and lie down, and he will tell you what to do" (v.4) uses yādaʿ — the rich Hebrew verb that encompasses intellectual knowledge, experiential knowledge, and intimate relationship (Gen 4:1). The ambiguity is intentional: Ruth will make herself known to Boaz as a covenant claimant, and Boaz — a man of integrity who already "knows" Ruth's character (2:11) — will respond with equal covenant faithfulness.

hist.context: Naomi's plan is bold and legally precise. She is not passive — she is engineering the redemption using the tools available to her. The instructions (wash, dress, approach at night, uncover feet, lie down) are unconventional but not improper. The threshing floor setting was public enough to be non-scandalous, private enough for a confidential proposal. Ruth's response ("I will do whatever you say") echoes the covenant commitment of 1:16.

refs.note: Naomi's recognition that Boaz is a gōʾēl (2:20) motivates this plan. The threshing floor encounter is not random romance but calculated legal strategy — Naomi knows the kinsman-redeemer obligation.

Levirate marriage law required a brother to marry his deceased brother's widow. Though Boaz is not a brother-in-law, the spirit of the levirate — raising up an heir for the dead — governs the entire narrative.

God tells Jerusalem: 'I spread the corner of my garment over you … I gave you my solemn oath and entered into a covenant with you.' Ruth's request — 'spread the corner of your garment over me' (3:9) — uses identical covenant-marriage language.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Naomi's instructions are remarkable for their detail and their lack of explanation. Alter notes she tells Ruth exactly what to do without telling her why or what to expect. The plan requires Ruth to trust completely — she does not know how Boaz will respond, what the uncovering means, or what she should say (Naomi says 'he will tell you what to do'). The plan's success depends entirely on Ruth's faithful execution of each step.

Naomi's instructions are detailed and precise — wash, anoint, dress, go down, do not make yourself known until he has finished eating and drinking, then go and uncover his feet and lie down. Alter notes the instructions leave Ruth completely in the dark about what to say: 'he will tell you what to do.' The plan requires total trust.

hubbard.source: Robert L. Hubbard Jr., NICOT Commentary on Ruth (1988) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Naomi's question — 'Shall I not seek security for you?' — uses the Hebrew menuchah (rest, security, settled-home). The same word appeared in Naomi's blessing at 1:9: 'May the LORD grant you rest (menuchah) in the home of another husband.' Hubbard notes Naomi is now the instrument of the very blessing she prayed for. Providence works through her deliberate action.

The threshing floor scene is set within the context of harvest celebration — a time of communal festivity, wine, and relaxed social boundaries. Hubbard notes that Naomi's instructions (wash, anoint, dress well) are preparation for a formal approach, not seduction. The uncovering of Boaz's feet and lying at them is a posture of legal petition — the supplicant at the feet of the one who can grant the petition.

Calvin on Naomi's plan: 'Naomi showed great prudence and affection in seeking a settlement for her daughter-in-law... She did not wait for Ruth to come of her own accord to ask her advice, but spontaneously applied her mind to seek what was expedient for her.' Naomi's initiative is proactive love — she does not wait to be asked; she acts for Ruth's welfare.

Calvin on uncovering the feet: 'This was a ceremony in use among the Hebrews, which indicated that a person placed himself under the protection of another. Thus Ruth humbly presented herself to Boaz, to receive whatever he should think proper to assign to her.' The action is legal and submissive, not sexual.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'put on your best clothes' (salmotayikh) discusses whether this implies the removal of widow's garments (as in Gen 38:14, where Tamar removes widow's clothes for the Judah encounter — another levirate parallel). The note suggests the primary meaning is simply her best available clothing, signalling she is presenting herself formally rather than as a field worker.

The NET note on uncovering Boaz's feet and lying down discusses the legal significance of the posture: the suppliant lying at the feet of the one who can grant a petition was a recognised legal gesture in ancient Near Eastern culture. Ruth is making a formal petition, not a casual approach. The act is deliberate and legally charged.

sections.header: Verses 7–13 — The Proposal: Kānāp; ʾĒšet Ḥayil; Boaz's Oath

heb.paragraph: Ruth's request that Boaz "spread the corner of his garment over her" (v.9) uses the same word Boaz used in his blessing (2:12) — "may you be repaid by the LORD God of Israel, under whose wings you have come to take refuge." Ruth is asking Boaz to become the human instrument of his own prayer. The kānāp is simultaneously the symbol of marriage (Ezek 16:8) and of divine protection — Boaz spreading it over Ruth is both a marriage proposal acceptance and a covenant-protective act.

heb.gloss: woman of noble character / valiant woman
heb.paragraph: Boaz's declaration that "all the people of my town know that you are a woman of noble character" (v.11) uses the precise phrase of Prov 31:10 — the book's concluding portrait of ideal womanhood. The term ḥayil, used of Boaz himself in 2:1, is now attributed to Ruth. The narrator is completing a theological portrait: these two — man and woman of ḥayil — are matched not by accident but by character, and the town's corporate witness confirms it.

Boaz's acknowledgment that there is "a kinsman-redeemer closer than I" (v.12) introduces the legal complication that structures chapter 4. The verb gāʾal carries full legal weight: redemption of property, redemption of the widow, perpetuation of the dead man's name and line. Boaz does not sidestep the legal precedence of the nearer kinsman — his integrity requires that the law be followed even when it might cost him Ruth. This is ḥesed enacted in legal form.

hist.context: The threshing floor scene is the book's most delicate. Ruth's proposal is legally precise (invoking the gōʾēl obligation) and personally courageous (choosing an older man of standing when she could have sought younger suitors). Nothing improper occurs. Boaz responds with legal precision — he accepts, praises her ḥesed, acknowledges the complication (a nearer redeemer exists), and makes a binding oath. The scene is governed entirely by honour.

refs.note: God spreads his kānāp over Israel in covenant marriage — the same gesture Ruth requests of Boaz. The proposal is simultaneously personal and covenantal.
ʾēšet ḥayil — "who can find a woman of noble character?" Boaz applies the Proverbs 31 title to Ruth here, before it appears in Proverbs. Ruth is the living embodiment of the poem.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Ruth's request — 'spread your kanaf over me' — directly echoes Boaz's prayer in 2:12 ('under whose wings you have taken refuge'). Alter notes the verbal echo is the book's most elegant piece of narrative craft: Ruth is asking Boaz to become the answer to his own prayer. The kanaf that YHWH was asked to provide in ch.2 is now the kanaf that Boaz is asked to provide in ch.3. Human hesed is the medium of divine hesed.

Boaz's blessing of Ruth is structured as a comparison: her second hesed (pursuing Boaz rather than a younger man) is greater than her first (accompanying Naomi). Alter notes the comparison elevates Ruth: the woman who displayed remarkable loyalty in ch.1 has now displayed even more remarkable fidelity. Boaz recognises a pattern of character, not merely a single generous act.

hubbard.source: Robert L. Hubbard Jr., NICOT Commentary on Ruth (1988) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Ruth's request — 'spread your kanaf over me' — is the book's most theologically precise moment. Hubbard notes that spreading one's garment over a woman was a recognised gesture of betrothal in the ancient Near East (Ezek 16:8 uses the same imagery for God's covenant with Israel: 'I spread the corner of my garment over you and covered your nakedness'). Ruth is formally proposing within the covenant-marriage framework.

Boaz's designation of Ruth as eshet chayil ('woman of valor/character') echoes his own description as ish gibbor chayil (2:1). Hubbard notes the deliberate verbal parallel — the narrator has been preparing this moment since ch.2's introduction of Boaz. The two principal characters are moral equals, both characterised by chayil (strength, valor, excellence of character).

Calvin on the garment-spreading: 'She asks Boaz to cover her with his wing, meaning to take her under his protection as a husband. The metaphor is noble — it signifies that in marriage the husband becomes as it were a wing under which the wife reposes safely.' The marriage is simultaneously legal protection, covenant commitment, and personal shelter.

Calvin on eshet chayil: 'Boaz confirms what he had said about Ruth's virtue, by adding that the whole city knows her to be a woman of virtue. Thus the praise which good men bestow on virtue is the voice of God himself, since they judge according to his word.' Public virtue is covenant-community testimony to divine character.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on 'spread your wing/corner' (ufarasta khanafkha) connects to Ezek 16:8 as the betrothal gesture. The note explains that kanaf in this context means the corner of the outer garment — spreading it over someone is formally claiming them as one's own, a betrothal act. Ruth's request is legally precise: she is invoking the go'el obligation in its marital dimension.

The NET note on eshet chayil discusses the phrase's most famous occurrence in Prov 31:10–31, 'the woman of valor.' Applying this title to Ruth — a Moabite widow gleaner — is the book's most direct statement about the nature of moral excellence: it is not ethnic or social but characterological. Ruth embodies the ideal of Prov 31 before that ideal is articulated there.

heb.paragraph: Boaz's gift of six measures of barley (v.15-17) is a symbolic quantity. Six is one short of seven (completeness/rest/Sabbath) — but generous beyond ordinary gleaning. The gift signals that a covenant process has begun that is not yet complete. Naomi interprets it correctly: "he will not rest (šāqaṭ) until the matter is settled today" (v.18). The six measures are a promise-in-kind: the seventh measure of completeness (the marriage, the son, the restored family) is coming.

heb.gloss: to be quiet / to rest / to settle
heb.paragraph: Naomi's confident prediction that Boaz "will not rest" (v.18) until the matter is resolved uses a word that denotes the settled peace of resolution. The same root describes the land's rest from war in Joshua (Josh 11:23; 14:15). Naomi has moved from the bitter woman who asked to be called Māraʾ to a woman of remarkable theological confidence, reading the signs of Boaz's character and predicting, rightly, that his integrity will drive the narrative to its covenant conclusion.

The narrative's repeated "mother-in-law" language (ḥāmôt) throughout chapters 1-3 builds toward chapter 4's resolution, where the women of Bethlehem declare that Ruth "is better to you than seven sons" (4:15) and Naomi becomes ʾômenet (foster-mother) to the child. Ruth the Moabite, who had no place in Israel's covenant structure, becomes the conduit through which Naomi's "mother" status is restored — a remarkable reversal of the emptiness of 1:20-21.

hist.context: The six measures of barley are the earnest — the legal pledge that the transaction will be completed. They are deliberately generous: Naomi asked Ruth to come back with a commitment, and Boaz sends her back with both a commitment and provision. The closing statement ("the man will not rest until the matter is settled today") is Naomi's confidence speaking — her theology has fully recovered from the lament of ch.1.

refs.note: I came back empty — rēqām. Boaz's words in v.17 ("don't go back empty-handed") directly reverse Naomi's complaint. The emptiness of ch.1 is being filled.
The man will not rest until the matter is settled today — and the very next verse opens with Boaz going up to the gate. Naomi's confidence is immediately vindicated.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The chapter's closing movement — Boaz's six-measure gift, Ruth's return with the grain, Naomi's confident 'sit still and wait' — resolves the tension of the threshing floor scene with quiet confidence. Alter notes the narrative pacing is exact: no more dialogue after Naomi's final speech, no action — just the waiting that Naomi names as the appropriate response. The reader, like Naomi, must wait for ch.4.

The chapter closes with two acts of stillness: Boaz sending Ruth back before the city stirs (protecting her reputation), and Naomi instructing Ruth to 'sit still' and wait. Alter notes the double stillness is the narrative's wisdom: the active phases (the threshing floor approach, Boaz's oath) are followed by patient waiting. Providence requires both bold action and patient trust.

hubbard.source: Robert L. Hubbard Jr., NICOT Commentary on Ruth (1988) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The six measures of barley Boaz gives Ruth are a deliberate act of communication. Hubbard notes the quantity is too large for a single woman to carry comfortably — it is a formal gift, a pledge, a token of intention. And his instruction — 'do not go to your mother-in-law empty' — shows that Boaz understands the full social and theological context. He is sending a message to Naomi as much as providing for Ruth.

Naomi's instruction to 'sit still' (shevi, literally 'dwell/remain') and wait for the outcome is the book's most significant shift in Naomi's character. Hubbard notes that in ch.1, Naomi was active in urging the women to leave; in ch.3 she becomes the one who waits confidently on another's action. Her active despair has been replaced by passive trust — a theological movement the book has been preparing from ch.2.

Calvin on the six measures: 'Boaz wished to testify his good-will to Naomi, and to encourage both of them to hope for a happy result.' The grain is a covenant sign — the physical substance of the promise Boaz has made. It is both practical provision and symbolic pledge. Calvin reads it as an act of complete transparency: Boaz has nothing to hide.

Calvin on 'the man will not rest': 'Naomi had observed that Boaz was a man of activity and decision — she therefore encourages her daughter-in-law to wait with patience, because Boaz would not be idle.' Confidence in Boaz's character grounds Naomi's confidence in the outcome. Faith in a person's integrity is itself a form of trust.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on six measures of barley (shesh se'orim) discusses the weight: six seahs is approximately 30 litres / 30 kg — an extremely heavy load for a woman to carry. The note suggests Boaz may have transported the grain himself to Naomi's door, or that Ruth carried it with difficulty. Either way, the quantity is a formal, substantial gift that signals serious intention.

The NET note on Naomi's question 'how did it go?' and Ruth's full account discusses the narrative function: Ruth's report to Naomi completes the chapter's frame. The six measures of grain are both practical provision and symbolic message — Boaz is signalling his commitment to the older woman's welfare as much as to Ruth's.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"Spread the corner of your garment over me, since you are a guardian-redeemer of our family."Ezek 16:8I spread the corner of my garment over you — God's covenant-marriage to Israel uses the same gesture. Ruth is asking Boaz to act as the human embodiment of divine covenant love.

src.title: Garment-spreading as proposal
src.quote: Ancient Near Eastern custom — a groom spreading his cloak over the bride as a covenant act. Ezek 16:8 uses it for God's covenant with Israel.
src.note: The gesture has clear legal and covenantal significance beyond romance.

src.title: Six measures
src.quote: The amount is not specified — barley is measured in seahs or ephahs. Whatever the exact quantity, it was enough that Naomi immediately asked "how did it go?" and Ruth needed to explain.

rec.text: The rabbis consistently read ch.3 as ṣniʿut (modesty): Ruth acted boldly but blamelessly. Boaz's commendation (v.11) is the narrative's verdict on the propriety of the entire scene.

In Jewish homes Prov 31:10–31 is sung to the wife on Friday night. Boaz's application of the title to Ruth in 3:11 is its most moving occurrence — a foreign widow recognised as the embodiment of the Proverbs 31 ideal.

rows.label: The Threshing Floor: Bold Initiative Under Cover of Night
rows.text: Naomi's precise instructions -- wash, anoint, dress, uncover his feet, lie down -- frame Ruth's act as deliberate legal petition, not casual approach. Spread the corner of your garment over me -- a marriage/redemption petition enacted.

rows.label: Boaz Responds: Honour and Hesed Recognised
rows.text: This hesed is greater than what you showed earlier: you have not run after the younger men. Boaz recognises the moral quality of Ruth's act -- she chose covenant loyalty over personal advantage. The town knows she is a woman of noble character.

hebtext.note: Naomi's desire to find "a home" (mānôaḥ) for Ruth (v.1) uses the same word she used of the LORD's gift to the daughters-in-law in 1:9 — "may the LORD grant you rest (mānôaḥ)." The word signifies more than shelter: it is covenant security, the settled condition of one whose future is secured. Naomi is now actively working to bring about what she had previously only prayed for — she has moved from lament to agency.

The threshing floor (v.2-3) is a liminal space in the OT — a place of agricultural abundance (Hos 9:1-2), of legal transaction (2 Sam 24:18-25), and in this narrative, of covenant negotiation. At night, after the celebratory harvest meal, the workers sleep on the threshing floor to guard the grain. Naomi's plan places Ruth in a space where legal-covenant action can occur, under cover of both darkness and the legitimate social custom of workers sleeping near the harvest.

Naomi's instruction that Ruth should "uncover his feet and lie down, and he will tell you what to do" (v.4) uses yādaʿ — the rich Hebrew verb that encompasses intellectual knowledge, experiential knowledge, and intimate relationship (Gen 4:1). The ambiguity is intentional: Ruth will make herself known to Boaz as a covenant claimant, and Boaz — a man of integrity who already "knows" Ruth's character (2:11) — will respond with equal covenant faithfulness.

Ruth's request that Boaz "spread the corner of his garment over her" (v.9) uses the same word Boaz used in his blessing (2:12) — "may you be repaid by the LORD God of Israel, under whose wings you have come to take refuge." Ruth is asking Boaz to become the human instrument of his own prayer. The kānāp is simultaneously the symbol of marriage (Ezek 16:8) and of divine protection — Boaz spreading it over Ruth is both a marriage proposal acceptance and a covenant-protective act.

The six measures are a promise-in-kind: the seventh measure of completeness (the marriage, the son, the restored family) is coming.

thread.text: God spreads his kānāp over Israel in covenant marriage — the same gesture Ruth requests of Boaz. The proposal is simultaneously personal and covenantal.
Boaz applies the Proverbs 31 title to Ruth, showing she embodies the ideal described in that earlier poem.
I came back empty — rēqām. Boaz's words in v.17 ("don't go back empty-handed") directly reverse Naomi's complaint. The emptiness of ch.1 is being filled.
The man will not rest until the matter is settled today — and the very next verse opens with Boaz going up to the gate. Naomi's confidence is immediately vindicated.

tx.title: Naomi's instructions — euphemistic language
tx.content: MT uses 'uncover his feet' (wĕgillît margĕlōtāyw), which is widely understood as a euphemism for the genital area (cf. Isa 7:20, 2 Sam 11:8). The LXX translates as ἀποκαλύψεις τὰ πρὸς ποδῶν αὐτοῦ ('uncover the area near his feet'), partially preserving the ambiguity. Targum Ruth removes all ambiguity: 'uncover the covering at his feet.'
tx.note: Whether margĕlōt is a genuine euphemism or a literal reference to feet remains debated. The ancient versions split: the LXX hints at more, the Targum insists on less.

tx.title: Ketiv/Qere — 'if' or 'surely'
tx.content: MT has a famous ketiv/qere split: the written text (ketiv) reads 'if indeed I am a redeemer' (conditional), while the marginal reading (qere) reads 'indeed I am a redeemer' (declarative — kî ʾāmnām without the conditional ʾim). The LXX reflects the qere reading. The SP is not relevant (Ruth is not in the Samaritan canon).
tx.note: The ketiv/qere system itself is a textual-critical apparatus built into the MT tradition. This instance shows how a single consonant changes the emotional tenor of Boaz's entire response.

debate.title: The Morality of the Threshing Floor Scene: Naomi's Plan and Ruth's Action

positions.name: Entirely proper: a legal marriage proposal
positions.argument: The scene is structured as a formal go'el marriage petition. The uncovering of feet, the lying at his feet, the request to spread his kanaf — all are elements of a recognised legal proposal, not a sexual encounter. The narrator's silence about any impropriety is evidence of propriety. Boaz's immediate concern for Ruth's reputation (3:14) confirms the scene was understood as legally, not sexually, charged.

positions.name: Ambiguous: the text deliberately leaves the night ambiguous
positions.argument: The scene deliberately uses vocabulary with sexual connotations (feet can be a euphemism, the darkness, the secrecy). The ambiguity is the point — the narrator leaves open what happened in order to focus on Ruth and Boaz's character: whatever happened, both acted honourably.
